devotional.title: Foundations and Structures
devotional.content:
{AtlasWarmE}{veryone}
who hears these words of mine and puts them into practice is like a wise 
man who built his house on the rock. The rain came down, the streams 
rose, and the winds blew and beat against that house; yet it did not 
fall, because it had its foundation on the rock.

A polynomial is built term by term. The constant provides a foundation. 
Higher-degree terms add complexity. The result is a complete structure 
that can model remarkably varied behaviors.

We have learned to take polynomials apart (factoring) and put them 
together (multiplication). We find their zeros—the places where they 
equal nothing—and use these to understand the whole. We trace their 
end behavior, seeing where they ultimately point.

All of mathematics is like this: building carefully, understanding deeply, 
finding the roots that anchor everything.
devotional.reflection:
What are the "zeros" in your life—the places 
where things balance to nothing? What are the foundations you're building 
upon? Where is your life ultimately pointing?

epigraph.text:
I have told you these things, so that in me you may 
have peace.

items:
A **polynomial** of degree $n$ has the form 
 $f(x) = a_n x^n + \cdots + a_1 x + a_0$ with $a_n \neq 0$

**Factoring techniques:** GCF, grouping, trinomials, special 
 patterns (difference of squares, sum/difference of cubes)

**Factor Theorem:** $c$ is a zero of $f(x)$ iff $(x-c)$ is 
 a factor

**Rational Root Theorem:** If $\frac{p}{q}$ is a rational 
 zero, then $p | a_0$ and $q | a_n$
**Division methods:** Long division and synthetic division

**Remainder Theorem:** The remainder when dividing by 
 $(x-c)$ equals $f(c)$

**Fundamental Theorem of Algebra:** A degree $n$ polynomial 
 has exactly $n$ zeros (counting multiplicity, including complex)

**End behavior:** Determined by degree (even/odd) and 
 leading coefficient (positive/negative)

**Multiplicity:** Affects whether graph crosses or touches 
 the $x$-axis
